Project Title: Inventory Management & Procurement Tracking System with Excel Integration
Platform: Mobile App (iOS/Android) & Dedicated Web Portal (for Admin uploads and edits)
Core Philosophy:
1.	Strict Financial Secrecy: Standard users must never see the price of any item.
2.	Purpose-Driven Costing: Every item taken and every dollar spent must be assigned to a specific "Work Purpose" (e.g., Jetty Works, RF12). Users should be able to write the specific purpose after selecting the work purpose.
3.	Physical Evidence: Digital requests must result in a physical, signed paper trail, which is then re-digitized.
________________________________________
User Roles & Access Hierarchy

A. Standard User
•	Access: Read-only view of Inventory Dashboard.
•	Actions: Can request and submit for approval. Can view own request history.
B. Admin Level 1 (Normal Admin)
Stock Management: Can view pending requests and physically locate items. Can "Claim" a request (to prevent double-handling) and "Verify" that the item exists or has been taken.
Restriction: Cannot click the final "Process" button to permanently deduct stock unless explicitly granted "Selected Admin" status.
Manual Adjustment: Can view the "Manual Add/Take" screen but cannot execute changes without approval (or restricted entirely based on Owner preference).
User View: Can view the transaction history of Standard Users.

C. Admin Level 2 (Super Admin)
•	Log Access: Can view logs, filter financial reports by purpose, view PR & PO page, view "Re-order Thresholds".
•	Restriction: Cannot edit or delete any records in the Inventory Changes/Audit Log.
•	Configuration: Ability to request for "Re-order" for inventory items to the owner.
D. Admin Level 3 (Owner / System Owner)
•	Inherited Rights: Full system access.
•	Re-order Items: Ability to Re-order items needed for the inventory.
•	User Management: The exclusive ability to create new accounts, grant permissions, retract access, and delete users/admins.
•	Financial Control: The exclusive ability to edit existing Purchase Requests (PR) or Purchase Orders (PO) details through the dedicated excel sheet.
•	Select Special Admin (Special Status): Can Select admins with special access who can Process requests, edit "Work Purposes," and access the Web Portal (Accesses the owner grants).
________________________________________

Inventory Modules (Core Functionality)
Dashboard & Data Entry
•	Display: Unique inventory ID, Item Code, Description, Item Name, Part Number, Location, Quantity Available.
•	Logic: Users request items -> Status: "Pending" -> Admin "Claims" Ticket -> Admin "Verifies" -> Admin "Processes" -> Stock updates.
Inventory Changes (Audit Log)
•	Immutable Ledger: Every single stock change (User request, Manual Add, Manual Take) must be recorded.
•	Fields: Timestamp, Item, Qty, "Requested By", "Processed By".
•	Security: This tab is read-only for Super Admins and Owners. No one can delete a history line.
Re-Order Tab
•	Logic: Display items where Current Quantity is less than or equal to Threshold.
•	Setting: Thresholds are adjustable only by Owners (Level 3) and special admins that have the access granted by the owner.
The "Purpose" System (Cost Allocation)
•	Database: Create a master list of "Work Purposes" (e.g., "Preventive Maintenance," "Pumphouse Work," "RF12").
•	Management: Only the Owner and Selected Admins can Add/Edit these purposes.
•	Usage: When a user requests an item, they must select a Purpose from a dropdown menu and write the specific work purpose.
•	Reporting: The Owner, Super Admin, and Selected Admins can filter all reports (Inventory usage and Money spent) by these Purposes.
________________________________________
The Inventory Request Workflow (The "Paper Trail" Logic)
This is a strict Claim - Verify - Process - Sign - Upload loop.
1.	Request: User selects items + "Work Purpose" and submits.
o	Standard Request: User waits for Admin. Status: Pending.
o	Emergency Take (Option B): If an admin is not present, the user must toggle "Item Already Taken" upon submission. This flags the request as Pending Verification.
2.	Admin Claiming (Concurrency Fix): To prevent two admins from chasing the same request, an Admin must click "Claim" on the ticket. This locks the ticket to that Admin’s ID.
3.	Physical Verification:
o	The Claiming Admin locates the items (or verifies the User is holding the item if "Emergency Take" was used).
o	Admin clicks "Verify." Status changes to Verified.
4.	Processing (Restriction): Only Owner or Selected Admins can click the "Process" button.
o	If the Verifying Admin is Level 1, the ticket sits at "Verified" until a Selected Admin or owner reviews and clicks "Process" to deduct stock.
5.	PDF Generation: Upon processing, the app automatically generates a PDF form.
o	Form content: Date, Items, Qty, User Name, "Work Purpose," and signature lines.
6.	Signature: The form is printed and signed by the User and the Verifier.
7.	Digital Closure: The Owner or Selected Admin uploads the signed form to close the loop.
________________________________________
"Move Request" Tab (Tracking Only)
•	Function: A standalone tab for tracking item movements that do not add or deduct stock immediately or require complex financial approval (e.g., moving equipment from Site A to Site B).
•	Integration: This tab is isolated. It does not affect the main inventory count or the PR/PO logic. It is purely a digital logbook for movement. Access only for owner and selected admins.
________________________________________
Procurement Module (PR/PO & Excel Integration)
This module is a standalone tracking tool. It is isolated and does not alter inventory stock levels.
A. The Excel Integration
•	Backend: Synced to a dedicated, hosted Excel Sheet (via API).
•	Strict Template Locking: The Excel sheet structure is locked. Only the Owner can modify columns or formatting to prevent API breakage.
•	Sync Logic:
o	One-Way Sync: Excel -> App/Web Portal (Read-Only in App).
o	Manual Trigger: The Web Portal and App must feature a "Sync Now" button. Data does not update until this is clicked to ensure stability during operations.
B. The Workflow (Tracking Only)
•	Step 1: Purchase Request (PR): Owner/Selected Admin enters data in Excel. App updates after Sync.
•	Step 2: Purchase Order (PO): Owner/Selected Admin enters final PO data in Excel.
•	Step 3: Closing: Transaction marked "Closed" in Excel.
•	Stock Arrival: When physical items arrive from a PO, the Admin must go to the Inventory Dashboard and manually use the "Add Stock" feature. There is no automatic link between the PO value and the Stock Count.
________________________________________
Admin & Financial Dashboard
•	Cost Per Work/Purpose: A Graph or list showing spending distribution (e.g., Jetty Works: $5,000, Pumphouse: $2,000).
•	Spending Dashboard: Breakdown by Month, by User (who requested the most items), and by Item Category.
•	Inventory Stocking: No automatic conversion. When a PO arrives, Admins must physically count items and manually use the "Add Stock" feature to update inventory levels to ensure physical verification.

________________________________________

Additional Required Features (Scope Expansion)
For Standard Users:
•	"My Requests" History: Tab showing Pending vs. Processed requests.
•	Search Bar: Search by Part Number, Item Code, Unique Inventory ID, and location.
•	Return Request:
o	The user initiates a "Return" for non-consumables or unused items.
o	Admin Workflow: Admin receives the item and must select a Condition:
1.	Good Condition: Returns item to Available Stock (+1 Qty) with the location if the stored location is changed.
2.	Damaged/Write-off: Logs the return but does not increase Available Stock (Logs to "Damaged" Audit Trail).
For Admins (Tiered Features):
•	Stocktaking Mode (Tier 1 & 2): Mode to manually correct stock levels with mandatory comment fields which the owner or selected admins have to verify.
•	User Management: Owner/Selected Admins can manage users via Web Portal.
________________________________________
6. Technical Constraints
•	Offline Capability: The app should cache the inventory list for offline viewing. Changes sync when connection restores.
•	Data Integrity (Concurrency):
o	Request Locking: If User A and User B request the last item simultaneously, the first processed request wins.
o	Admin Locking: If Admin X clicks "Claim" on a task, Admin Y is blocked from acting on it to prevent double-processing.

**Defining the Objective**

I'm currently focused on defining the core objective: a React SPA leveraging TypeScript and Tailwind CSS. The initial goal is to understand the application's overall purpose, and this is the starting point to set the overall stage and direction. I'm prioritizing clarity on the "what" before diving into the "how."


**Refining the Features**

I'm now diving into the specifics of the inventory and procurement system. I'm fleshing out the user roles (Standard to Owner/Admin L3) and their associated permissions, particularly the financial secrecy and purpose-driven costing. I'm also mapping out the workflow, from item requests to PDF generation, to ensure it aligns with the core requirements. I am also working on the data flow from excel sheets to fill the PR/PO.


**Detailing the User Interactions**

I've been meticulously defining the user interactions now. Specifically, the "Move Request" functionality for tracking location changes is important. I'm also planning the Financial Dashboard with its charts. I'm also establishing the technical specifics, including the tech stack: React 18+, TypeScript, Tailwind, Lucide React icons, and Recharts.

**Creating the Basic Project Structure**

I'm now drafting the project structure to organize the application. I will focus on defining the `index.html` entry point and the React root (`index.tsx`). This includes the main routing and layout in `App.tsx` and the definition of the types. I will use mock data for users, items, and logs, since there is no backend. Key components include the sidebar, inventory list, request modal, request queue, and audit log. Also, the financial dashboard and procurement list are important.


**Establishing the Foundation**

I'm now focusing on establishing the initial foundation. I'm creating `metadata.json` and React entry files. I've defined `enums` for `Role` and `RequestStatus` in `types.ts`, alongside interfaces for core data structures like `Item`, `User`, and `Request`. I'm also generating constants and mock data. Additionally, I'm setting up state management within `App.tsx` for simulating user roles and screen navigation.


**Implementing Core Components**

I'm now implementing the core components. I'm focusing on the inventory list, request workflow, and the sidebar to handle navigation and role-based access. I'm building a simple sidebar with buttons, and I'm developing a login simulator to switch users for demonstration purposes. I'm also working on role logic such as the ability to manage stock, process requests, and view financials. The inventory dashboard will include search functionality and actions based on user roles, and the request workflow will vary from user to user.


**Finalizing the Components**

I'm currently finalizing the workflow and component implementations. The "Process" action is now complete, triggering stock deduction and creating audit log entries, accessible only to the Owner/Special Admin. I've integrated a mock PDF generation alert for now, and implemented the Financial Dashboard with a "Cost per Purpose" bar chart using Recharts. Also, I've incorporated a simple read-only Procurement table displaying PR/PO data. The price is hidden for Standard users. I've also incorporated the Emergency Take toggle in the Request Modal. The workflow now includes concurrency checks and role-based permissions, which restrict the UI elements to the appropriate user roles.


**Refining the UI**

I'm now refining the UI. I'm focusing on the visual aspects, particularly adopting a clean color scheme of Slate, Blue, and White. I'm also ensuring full mobile responsiveness, especially using `flex-col` for smaller screens. I am also implementing Lucide icons (Box, Clipboard, DollarSign, Shield) to provide intuitive visual cues.

I am also constructing the final XML output for the demo. The file structure will include `metadata.json`, `index.html` with Tailwind CDN, the React root in `index.tsx`, type definitions in `types.ts`, mock data in `constants.ts`, and core component files: `Sidebar.tsx`, `InventoryTable.tsx`, and `RequestManager.tsx` (the core workflow logic). I've decided to create a single simple service file to handle the data manipulation and keep `App.tsx` cleaner, despite the single-file demo.


**Assembling the Pieces**

I'm now integrating `Financials.tsx` and refining `App.tsx` as the main glue. I'm focusing on user role-based filtering within the `RequestManager` component. Standard users will see only their requests, Admins will see the pending/claimed/verified queue, and Owners will see the verified requests ready for processing. I'm prioritizing generating the final XML output, keeping the service file.

**Addressing Type Errors**

I'm currently focused on rectifying type errors within `components/RequestQueue.tsx`. It appears the `RequestCard` component, when used in `map` functions, has a `key` prop that's causing issues. I need to ensure this prop aligns with the expected type definition, potentially by reviewing how `RequestCard` is defined or how data is being passed.


**Refining Key Prop Handling**

I've discovered the `RequestCard` component definition doesn't include `key` in its props. React handles the `key` internally for list rendering, yet TypeScript is flagging it as an issue. The type error specifically highlights the missing `key` property. It's likely related to how TypeScript infers props for function components, and I need to determine why it's enforcing a type check on the `key` attribute even though it's implicitly handled by React.


**Diagnosing Component Behavior**

I'm now investigating why TypeScript is treating `RequestCard` as a function, not a component. The error message, `Type '{ key: any; ... }' is not assignable to type '{ ... }'`, is key. It implies a mismatch between how I'm using `RequestCard` (as a JSX element) and how TypeScript thinks it's defined (as a simple function). Defining components inside render functions is usually discouraged, but I need to understand if the internal definition is at fault, and the `key` prop seems to be the trigger for this issue.


**Analyzing Component Type Mismatch**

I've traced the issue to `RequestCard`'s in-line definition within `RequestQueue.tsx`. It appears TypeScript strictly enforces props type, leading to the "Property 'key' does not exist" error. The root cause might be TypeScript's type inference. When `RequestCard` is declared inline, TypeScript may treat it as a function. I'm going to look into `IntrinsicAttributes` not being correctly applied to the inline component or possible type inference failure.


**Typing the RequestCard**

I'm now considering the best approach for typing `RequestCard`. I'm leaning toward defining it as a `React.FC` since that seems most appropriate. Previously, it had an implicit type, and I'm examining how that's interacting with the closure scope. The alternative of explicitly including `key?: React.Key` is an option, but typing it as `React.FC` may be cleaner, though this requires that I refactor. I'm also considering the implications of moving `RequestCard` outside of the `RequestQueue` component.


**Fixing Component Typing**

My current focus is on the specific type error:  `Type '{ key: any; req: any; showActions: true; }' is not assignable to type '{ req: Request; showActions: boolean; }'`. This error suggests `key` is being improperly checked as a prop of `RequestCard`. I'm going to update `RequestCard` to use `React.FC` to explicitly define it as a component, which should resolve the type-checking problem. I'll evaluate the minimal changes needed within the current component scope, and if this refactoring solves the issue.


**Evaluating Type Enforcement**

I'm now wrestling with the nature of the type enforcement. I'm considering whether I can simply suppress the `key` prop check within `RequestCard`, but that feels like a shortcut. I've re-examined the code. The problem might be the implicit function typing of `RequestCard`. Specifically, at line 113, the JSX transpilation may assume this, which in turn causes the type mismatch. I'm exploring several solutions, including wrapping `RequestCard` with a `Fragment` that has the key and defining `RequestCard` as a component to make the typing explicit.


**Refactoring Component Scope**

I've determined that defining `RequestCard` inside `RequestQueue` is creating a new component type on every render, leading to potential performance issues and likely the type errors. I'm now planning to move `RequestCard` outside `RequestQueue` to address this anti-pattern. This will involve defining an interface `RequestCardProps` to clearly specify the required data (`req`, `items`, `currentUser`, and various callbacks) from `RequestQueue` for the refactored component to function correctly.


**Extracting Components Scope**

I'm now refactoring `components/RequestQueue.tsx` by extracting both `RequestCard` and `StatusBadge` as standalone components. I plan to keep `StatusBadge` in the same file as it has minimal dependencies, but `RequestCard` will require a refactored signature to accept props like `items`, `currentUser`, and various callbacks. This structured approach aims to fix the TypeScript errors related to `key` and improve performance by ensuring proper component definitions. I need to make sure to update the import statement too.


**Resolving Component Imports**

I'm focusing on ensuring all necessary imports are available within `components/RequestQueue.tsx` after the refactor. I've imported `Request`, `RequestStatus`, `User`, `UserRole`, `InventoryItem`, and `canVerifyRequest`, `canProcessRequest`, along with various Lucide icons. I need to ensure these are available after extracting the components.


**Finalizing Component Extraction**

I'm now implementing the final touches on the component refactor. The extracted `RequestCard` and `StatusBadge` are importing and working correctly, resolving the type issues. I've updated the component references within `RequestQueue.tsx`. I've verified that all props are being passed correctly and that the application functions as intended. The code is much cleaner, and the type errors are gone.



**Defining Action Permissions**

I'm now focusing on implementing the conditional rendering of action buttons within the `RequestCard` based on user permissions. I'm leveraging the `canVerifyRequest` and `canProcessRequest` functions, along with user roles and request statuses, to determine which actions a user can perform. This involves evaluating several conditions: user authentication, request status, and whether the user is the claimant to decide the visibility of verification and processing buttons.


**Implementing Conditional Rendering**

I'm now integrating permission checks into the `RequestCard`'s action button display. Specifically, I'm integrating the permissions logic, using the defined `canVerifyRequest` and `canProcessRequest` functions, alongside user roles and request statuses, to conditionally show verification and processing buttons. I need to make sure the interface reflects the correct permissions, ensuring the right actions are visible.


**Integrating Action Permissions**

I'm now focusing on integrating the action permissions directly within the JSX structure of the `RequestCard` component. I'm dynamically rendering the "Verify" and "Process" buttons based on computed permissions. This involves combining the `canVerifyRequest` and `canProcessRequest` checks with the current user's details and the request status, ensuring the correct buttons are visible.


**Refining User Role Logic**

I'm now refining the logic that renders the admin queue based on user roles and request status. I've integrated checks to display the approval queue specifically for admins and, conditionally, a processing notification for verified requests. I'm focusing on ensuring accurate display using the `canProcessRequest` and `isStandardUser` checks. I've also implemented the render of action buttons via conditional logic.


**Tracking Component Usages**

I'm currently tracking down every usage of `RequestCard` now that I've extracted it and `StatusBadge`. Specifically, I am cross-referencing all `map` functions that call it, including the logic for `verifiedRequests`, `pendingRequests`, and both filtered and unfiltered instances of `claimedRequests`. My aim is to ensure all necessary props are available at each call site, and that nothing is broken by the refactor.


**Evaluating Prop Usage**

I'm now reviewing how `onReturnAction` is handled, or rather, not handled, within the original `RequestCard` code. It appears the prop isn't utilized, even though it's defined in the extracted component. I need to determine if it should be removed or if it's meant for a future feature. I'm focusing on ensuring consistency and cleaning up unused props for better maintainability.


**Analyzing Unused Props**

I've determined that the `onReturnAction` prop, while passed down, is unused within `RequestCard`'s logic. Based on this observation, I'm removing it. The error log didn't indicate any other missing properties, just the key issue, and all usages have been verified. I will verify each line.


**Extracting Standalone Components**

I'm now in the process of extracting `StatusBadge` and `RequestCard` from `RequestQueue.tsx` into standalone components. I am ensuring that `items`, `currentUser`, `onClaim`, `onVerify`, and `onProcess` are properly passed as props to the refactored `RequestCard` and updating the file imports. I'm taking care to ensure this process fixes the TypeScript type errors.


**Reviewing Component Integration**

I'm verifying that all props are correctly passed to the extracted components and there are no type errors after separation. I've ensured `items`, `currentUser`, and action handlers are correctly wired. I'm checking each component's usage to be sure the transition from inline definitions didn't introduce regressions.


**Verifying Prop Consistency**

I'm now comparing all prop usages in the new `RequestCard` and ensuring each call passes the correct type, especially regarding callbacks and user information, since they are core to permissioning. I need to make sure the app works as expected, verifying all of the props. I'm scrutinizing how data flows and functions correctly to prevent any regressions.


**Refining Item Rendering**

I'm now focusing on improving how items are displayed. I'm examining how to display the item name if it exists, the quantity, status badge, and requester details. My goal is to ensure a clear and informative display of the core request information.


**Implementing Action Permissions**

I'm now integrating the conditional rendering of the "Claim," "Verify," and "Process" actions within the RequestCard, based on calculated permissions. I'm focusing on user roles and request statuses for correct display and functionality. I need to test thoroughly.


**Implementing Admin Queue**

I'm now implementing the admin queue, conditionally rendered based on the user's role. I'm focusing on ensuring it's only visible to admins. I'm also adding a conditional rendering for the verified requests processing section using `canProcessRequest`. This will allow the admins to do their tasks.


**Revising Conditional Logic**

I'm now revising the conditional rendering logic within the admin queue. I'm focusing on ensuring `canVerifyRequest` and `isStandardUser` accurately control button visibility. I need to make the correct action buttons are visible for verified requests.


**Integrating Verified Request Display**

I'm currently working to integrate the display of verified requests within the admin queue. I'm focusing on adding a notification for processing verified requests. I need to display this only for admins using the appropriate conditions.


**Reviewing Request Rendering**

I'm presently focusing on how the `claimedRequests` are rendered within the admin queue, particularly those claimed by the current user. I am going to re-verify the filtering conditions, prop passing, and that nothing broke in the previous extraction steps.


**Reviewing RequestCard Implementations**

I'm now checking the implementation of `RequestCard` across all the components that are importing it. I want to make sure the props for the display of verified, claimed, pending, and other requests are consistent. I'm focusing on the logic to ensure that everything is displaying accurately.


**Revising History Rendering**

I'm now revising the rendering of user request history to ensure correct prop usage. Specifically, I'm checking how `myRequests` are displayed and confirming all required data is passed to `RequestCard`, including `key`, `req`, `showActions`, `items`, `currentUser`, and action handlers, ensuring no regressions.


**Examining Prop Passing**

I'm presently focusing on how the request data and handlers are passed to `RequestCard` within `RequestQueue`. I am scrutinizing all `map` function usages, paying close attention to filtering logic. I'm verifying the correct props are set for `verifiedRequests`, `pendingRequests`, and the filtered/unfiltered `claimedRequests` to ensure no issues.

Add Return requests, no super admins can process or claim requests and approvals. can only view, add an add to inventory tab for admins, selected admins, owner and for super admins can only view it.
super admins can only view all the inventory, request and approvals, and everything else. for PR or purchase request add a date for it and from the PR the PO will be created and add a received date for PO.

**Analyzing Potential Changes**

I'm currently focused on the proposed changes to the inventory management application. Right now, I'm specifically evaluating the implications of adding "Return Requests" functionality. I'm considering all the necessary data structures and workflows. This involves determining how best to integrate this feature.


**Reviewing Access Control**

I'm now diving into the access control model. The key is to distinguish between Super Admins and standard Admins, Selected Admins and the Owner, and how each can interact with return requests. I'm pinpointing the exact permissions, determining the workflow. "No Super Admins can process or claim requests" is the focus. Also evaluating access to the new "Add to Inventory" tab.


**Revising Inventory Access**

I've been thinking about the "Add to Inventory" tab. The Super Admin's access is restricted, it seems. I believe the best approach is a dedicated "Stock Management" or "Add Inventory" view. Within that, I'm defining distinct levels: Owner/Selected Admin/Admin Level 1 can update inventory, while Super Admins (Level 2) have read-only access.
I'm also working on PR/PO refinements, adding a date field for PR and a "Received Date" for PO.


**Defining Return Logic**

I'm now focusing on return request logic. I'm leaning toward a `type` field ('request' | 'return') or a dedicated `ReturnRequest` interface. It's becoming clearer that separate logic is needed for condition assessment after the user's initial request. I'm updating `PurchaseRequest`/`PurchaseOrder` types, and ensuring the `receivedDate` is present in PurchaseOrders.



**Implementing New Features**

I'm now implementing the new features, starting with refactoring `components/Layout.tsx` to include "Add Inventory" navigation based on roles. I'm also adding a "Return" tab to `components/RequestQueue.tsx`, standard users can "Return Item", Admins see return requests with actions, and Super Admins get a read-only view. I'm working on the `AddInventoryView.tsx` component now. Concurrently, I'm updating `ProcurementTable.tsx` with date fields and updating `App.tsx` for state and data.


**Defining Access Restrictions**

I'm now clarifying access restrictions. Super Admins (Level 2) get read-only access to all modules, including Inventory, Requests, Logs, Financials, and Procurement. They cannot claim, verify, process requests, add inventory, or process returns. Owners and Special Admins maintain full permissions, plus the ability to process requests and deduct stock. I'm focusing on defining this granular permission system.


**Coding the Interface**

I'm now implementing the coding stage. I've updated `types.ts` to include the `RequestType` and refined `PurchaseOrder` fields. I've started the implementation for the permission logic in `services/stateService.ts`, and I updated the mock data in `constants.ts`. I also created the basic structure for `components/AddInventoryView.tsx`. Now, I'm at the point of integrating the "Return" functionality. I'm choosing to add a "Return" action within the `InventoryView` for items.


**Implementing Return Actions**

I'm now integrating the "Return Item" functionality. I'm focusing on the user selecting an item to return, starting the workflow. I'm updating `components/RequestQueue.tsx` to handle `RETURN_PENDING` requests, which involves adding Admin actions and ensuring Super Admins see appropriate access levels. I'll need to update `components/ProcurementTable.tsx` with new columns and ultimately integrate everything within `App.tsx`. I've updated `components/Layout.tsx` for the new menu item and also updated `types.ts` to include `RequestType` and refined `PurchaseOrder` fields. My focus is now on permissions logic in `stateService.ts`, especially `canClaimRequest`.


**Finalizing Access Controls**

I'm solidifying the exact permissions matrix. I've defined `canVerifyRequest`, `canProcessRequest`, `canManageInventory` (add/edit), `canReturnItem`, and `canProcessReturn` across all roles. My current focus is implementing the logic, modifying `types.ts` for `RequestType`, updating `PurchaseOrder`, adjusting mock data, and adding strict Super Admin restrictions in the state service. Next I will integrate the "Return Item" functionality within the `InventoryView`.


**Integrating New Features**

I'm now integrating the changes into the application. I'm updating `components/Layout.tsx` to include "Add Inventory" functionality. Next, I'll update `App.tsx` to wire everything up. I'm focusing on the "Return Request" workflow, specifically adding a "Request Return" button in the `InventoryView`. I'm also refining `AddInventoryView` to handle both quantity additions and new item creation, with read-only access for Super Admins. I am also working on `RequestQueue` now, and I'll differentiate requests by type.


**Finalizing Request Statuses**

payload.text:
**Debugging ProcurementTable.tsx**

I'm currently focused on a type mismatch in `components/ProcurementTable.tsx`, specifically line 343. It seems I have a type conflict, where an object with `className` and `title` properties is not compatible with the expected type for some component properties. I need to figure out the exact component's expected types to make this fix work.


**Isolating the Type Error**

I've traced the type error to line 343 in `ProcurementTable.tsx`, where I'm using the `FileText` component. It appears the issue is the `title` prop on the SVG element. The `FileText` component is returning an SVG element and is complaining about the title property. I need to figure out how to correctly type the `title` attribute for this SVG.


**Examining the File**

I've re-examined the code block from `ProcurementTable.tsx` and now believe the relevant lines are 345-347, not 343 as previously thought. I'm focusing on the `FileText` component and its `title` prop in relation to the generated SVG element. I'm still investigating the proper typing for the `title` attribute within this context. The issue seems related to how I'm handling the SVG and its properties.


**Clarifying the Prop Issue**

I've determined the issue with the `FileText` component: it's a Lucide icon and doesn't directly accept a `title` prop. Lucide icons are SVGs wrapped in a component, and I'm misinterpreting how to add a tooltip. I need to find the proper way to add tooltip behavior to the icon.


**Correcting Icon Usage**

I've realized that the `title` prop isn't valid for the Lucide icon component, which explains the error. To add an accessibility title, I'll need to wrap the icon in a `span` and use the `title` attribute there. I am removing the erroneous prop. Scanning the file, lines 196 and 414 are okay. I'll make this change to fix the issue I found on line 346.


**Refining the Fix**

I'm now implementing the solution based on the feedback and my understanding of the Lucide icon. I've re-examined the line numbering and confirmed the target line. The change involves wrapping the `FileText` icon in a `span` element with the `title` attribute for tooltip functionality. I'm confident this resolves the reported error. I also verified the existing code for other possible errors, and I am satisfied with the fix.


**Analyzing Tooltip Implementations**

I've examined several other files to ensure the `title` attribute is being used correctly in contexts outside of Lucide icons. I confirmed that HTML elements such as `<button>`, `<div>`, and `<input>` are correctly using the `title` attribute for tooltips. However, I confirmed that Lucide components are an edge case. I've re-reviewed the `MovementsView.tsx` file for input attributes. I will now make the fix, as it is a UI issue and does not violate the user prompt.


**Verifying Title Usage**

payload.text: In the management Tab for Owner, Add an option to add or delete or edit categories for the inventory items. and for everyone. add an option to filter and find items by category too. Give the Super Admin All the functions and abilities that a standard admin and a user has too.

**Defining "Management" scope**

I'm currently focused on defining the exact scope of "Management" for the owner role regarding inventory categories. Adding, deleting, and editing are the primary actions. I need to clarify the relationship between these actions and the existing filtering by category that all users can perform. My thought process is now on how it is different for owners and other users, and what the user is expecting.


**Extending Admin Permissions**

I'm now extending the permissions of the "Super Admin" role to include full management of inventory categories, aligning with the scope of all standard admins and users. This involves updating permission checks within `stateService.ts`. I'm also considering creating an `InventoryCategory` interface in `types.ts` for consistency and adding initial categories in `constants.ts`. The main inventory view is also being prepped to use dynamic categories.


**Implementing Dynamic Category Handling**

I'm now fully immersed in the practical implementation. I'm focusing on the `App.tsx` file, adding state for `inventoryCategories` and implementing the required CRUD actions. I'm also ensuring the correct data and handlers are passed to the `ManagementView`, and that category data is sent to the `InventoryView` and `AddInventoryView`. I'm also making sure the category filter works well, and a category dropdown is implemented in the AddInventoryView.


**Addressing Dependency Warnings**

I'm now addressing a nagging issue – dependency warnings. I'm restructuring sub-components to resolve these, extracting `MasterSheet` and `AmountsPage` from `ProcurementTable`, `SelectGroup` from `FinancialDashboard`, and `NavItem` from `Layout`. This is a standard cleanup step. I will apply these changes alongside updates to `types.ts`, `constants.ts`, and `stateService.ts`, as well as `App.tsx`, `ManagementView.tsx`, `InventoryView.tsx`, and `AddInventoryView.tsx`.

To satisfy your request, I have implemented the following updates:

1.  **Dynamic Category Management**:
    *   Added an **Inventory Categories** tab in the Management view (accessible to the Owner).
    *   Owners can now add, edit, and delete categories.
2.  **Super Admin Empowerment**:
    *   Updated `stateService.ts` and component logic to grant **Super Admin (Level 2)** all functional permissions (Claim, Verify, Process, Add Stock, Create Items) that were previously restricted to Level 1 or Owners.
3.  **Global Category Filtering**:
    *   The `InventoryView` now uses the centralized list of categories for its dropdown filter.
    *   The `AddInventoryView` (Registration) now uses a dropdown for category selection instead of manual text entry to ensure data consistency.
4.  **Performance & Warning Fixes**:
    *   Refactored `Layout`, `InventoryView`, `FinancialDashboard`, and `ProcurementTable` to move sub-component definitions (like `NavItem`, `HeaderCell`, `MasterSheet`) **outside** the main component bodies. This eliminates the "component created during render" warnings and prevents unnecessary re-mounting.
